coredata.dc:title: Hydrogen peroxide splitting on Nafion-coated graphene quantum dots/carbon nitride photocatalysts
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Shiraishi, Yasuhiro
Soramoto, Airu
Ichikawa, Satoshi
Tanaka, Shunsuke
Hirai, Takayuki
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has received growing interest as an energy carrier. For the development of a sustainable energy society using H2O2 as a hydrogen carrier, the photocatalytic H2O2 splitting into H2 and O2 is a desirable reaction for the generation of H2. This reaction, however, is difficult to promote because the photoexcited electrons reduce H2O2 to H2O more preferably than the H+ reduction, thus suppressing the H2 generation. Here we report that visible light irradiation of Nafion-coated graphene quantum dots/carbon nitride (Nf-QD/CN) photocatalysts promotes the H2O2 splitting. The surface Nf layer on the catalysts behaves as an H2O2-impermeable/H+-permeable membrane to promote the H+ reduction while suppressing the H2O2 reduction, resulting in the H2 generation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen peroxide
Photocatalysis
Hydrogen
Carbon nitride
Nafion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: LSV curves (scan rate 0.1 V s−1) obtained at (a) negative and (b) positive potentials on the respective catalysts loaded on a glassy carbon (GC) electrode in 10 mM H2O2 solutions containing 0.1 M Na2SO4 under N2 atmosphere.
TEM images and EDS maps of the C and F elements for the respective catalysts. The arrows represent the QDs.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism on the surface of the respective catalysts.

The amount of H2 generated and the H2 selectivity during photoirradiation of λ > 420 nm light in a closed gas circulation system (3 kPa Ar). Reaction conditions: catalyst (0.2 g), H2O2 (10 mM, 0.1 L), H3PO4 (0 or 1 M), temperature (293 K), light irradiation (solar simulator with AM 1.5G filter, 1-sun). The H2 selectivity was calculated using the Eq. (9).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: [a]
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: catalyst (0.2 g), H2O2 solution (10 mM, 0.1 L), temperature (293 K), solar simulator (1-sun, AM 1.5G filter), time (6 h).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: [c]
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Determined by redox titration with KMnO4.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: [d]
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: H2O2 solution (10 mM, 0.1 L) with H3PO4 (1 M).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: [e]
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Catalysts were reused after simple washing with water.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: [f]
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: H2O2 solution (10 mM, 0.1 L) with 0.2 mL of an Nf dispersion solution, which contains the identical amount of Nf to that in entry 4.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Total elemental composition of the catalysts.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: [c]
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Determined from the difference of other elements.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: [d]
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Recovered after 6 h of photoreaction (Table 1, entry 4).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface elemental composition of the catalysts.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: [a]
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Determined by the XPS charts with the atomic sensitivity factors according to ref [23] (C 1 s, 1.00; N 1 s, 1.22; O 1 s, 2.85; F 1 s, 4.26; S 2p, 0.59).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: [c]
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Recovered after 6 h of photoreaction (Table 1, entry 4).

head.ce:title: Hydrogen peroxide splitting on Nafion-coated graphene quantum dots/carbon nitride photocatalysts

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Solar Energy Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The H2O2 splitting into H2 and O2 can be promoted by visible light irradiation of Nafion-coated graphene quantum dots/carbon nitride (Nf-QD/CN) photocatalysts. The surface Nf layer behaves as an H2O2-impermeable/H+-permeable membrane to promote the H2 generation.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has received growing interest as an energy carrier. For the development of a sustainable energy society using H2O2 as a hydrogen carrier, the photocatalytic H2O2 splitting into H2 and O2 is a desirable reaction for the generation of H2. This reaction, however, is difficult to promote because the photoexcited electrons reduce H2O2 to H2O more preferably than the H+ reduction, thus suppressing the H2 generation. Here we report that visible light irradiation of Nafion-coated graphene quantum dots/carbon nitride (Nf-QD/CN) photocatalysts promotes the H2O2 splitting. The surface Nf layer on the catalysts behaves as an H2O2-impermeable/H+-permeable membrane to promote the H+ reduction while suppressing the H2O2 reduction, resulting in the H2 generation.

ce:section.ce:para:
Artificial photosynthesis, the solar-driven transformation of earth-abundant resources into fuels, is a challenging issue for the establishment of a sustainable energy society. A variety of photoreactions such as overall water splitting (H2/O2 generation) [1], CO2 reduction (CO or HCOOH generation) [2], and N2 reduction (NH3 generation) [3] have been studied extensively. Recently, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has received increasing attention as a fuel because it is a storable and transportable liquid, it generates electricity in a direct peroxide–peroxide fuel cell [4], and it can be generated from water and O2 by photocatalysis (Eq. (1)) [5]. Recently, we demonstrated that the resorcinol–formaldehyde resin metal-free semiconductor photocatalysts successfully absorb ∼700 nm light and generate H2O2 with >0.5 % of solar-to-chemical energy conversion efficiencies [6–8], which are higher than those of powder catalysts used in the artificial photosynthesis reactions [1–3]. Therefore, H2O2 is a potentially promising liquid solar fuel.
(1)
H 2
O + 1 / 2
O 2
→
h ν
H 2
O 2
( Δ G ° = + 117
kJ
mol
- 1
)

Towards a sustainable energy society using H2O2 as an energy carrier, the on-site generation of H2 from a H2O2 solution (Eq. (2)) is an essential technology. Theoretically, the up-hill H2O2 splitting reaction can be promoted by the photocatalysis consisting of the oxidation of H2O2 by the valence band holes (VB h+) (Eq. (3)) and the reduction of H+ by the conduction band electrons (CB e–) (Eq. (4)). There has, however, been no report of a successful system for this reaction because: (i) H2O2 is decomposed into water and O2 by the disproportionation on the surface of conventional metal/metal oxide-based catalysts (Eq. (5)) [9]; (ii) H2O2 is also decomposed by absorbing ultraviolet (UV) light (Eq. (6)) [10]; and, (iii) the CB e– promote the H2O2 reduction (Eq. (7)) more efficiently than the H+ reduction (Eq. (4)) owing to its low-lying reduction potential [11]. The design of metal-free photocatalytic systems that selectively reduce H+ while suppressing the H2O2 reduction under visible light is necessary.
(2)
H 2
O 2
→
h ν
H 2
+
O 2
( Δ G ° = + 131
kJ
mol
- 1
)
(3) H2O2 → O2 + 2H+ + 2e– (+0.68 V vs. NHE)
(4) 2H+ + 2e– → H2 (0 V vs. NHE)
(5) H2O2 → H2O + 1/2O2 (ΔG° = –117 kJ mol−1)
(6)
H 2
O 2
→
h ν
λ < 400
nm
2 · OH
(7) H2O2 + 2H+ + 2e– → 2H2O (+1.76 V vs. NHE)

Recently, we reported a photocatalytic system promoting the H2O2 splitting [12]. We used a carbon nitride (CN) metal-free semiconductor powder [13], whose VB and CB levels lie at +2.00 and –0.63 V (vs. NHE), respectively, capable of oxidizing H2O2 (Eq. (3)) and reducing H+ (Eq. (4)). We loaded graphene quantum dots (QD), the newly-emerging non-metal co-catalysts for H2 generation [14], onto the CN surface. Visible light irradiation of the QD/CN powders suspended in an H2O2 solution containing phosphoric acid (H3PO4) successfully generates H2. The H2O2–H3PO4 hydrogen-bonding interaction suppresses the H2O2 reduction (Eq. (7)), thus promoting the H+ reduction (Eq. (4)). This system, however, requires very high concentrations of H3PO4 (∼1 M), which may become impurities when using the H2O2 solution for other purposes such as bleaching, disinfection, and electricity generation. The QD/CN photocatalytic system must therefore be improved to the one that does not use the H2O2 stabilizer (H3PO4) for the practical applications.

We focused on Nafion (Nf), the anionic perfluorosulfonic polymers that have widely been employed as an H+-permeable membrane in a fuel cell [15]. Its water-impermeable property led us to assume that the QD/CN catalyst if coated with Nf may suppress the H2O2 permeation while allowing the H+ permeation. In the present work, the QD/CN powders were coated with Nf by a simple stirring of the QD/CN powders in a commercially-available Nf dispersion solution. Visible light irradiation of the Nf-coated catalysts (Nf-QD/CN) successfully generates H2 from a H2O2 solution even without the use of H3PO4.

The QD/CN powder was prepared according to the procedure described previously [12]. The Nf x -QD/CN catalysts (x = 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.2, 1.6) were prepared as follows: the QD/CN powder (0.4 g) and x mL of a 5 wt% Nf dispersion solution (Sigma-Aldrich 527084-25ML, x = 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.2, 1.6 mL) were added to water (40 mL). The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h using a magnetic stirrer. Evaporation of the solvents by a rotary evaporator afforded brownish powders of Nf x -QD/CN.

Photocatalytic H2O2 splitting was performed in a closed gas circulation system connected to a gas chromatography equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (GC-TCD) [12,16]. Each of the respective catalysts (0.2 g) was added to a H2O2 solution (10 mM, 0.1 L) within a quartz cell. The cell was connected to a gas circulation system, and the system was depressurized to 3 kPa after the repeated Ar gas purging. The cell was immersed in a temperature-controlled water bath (±0.1 K) and photoirradiated using a solar simulator equipped with AM 1.5 G filter (SAN-EI Electric Inc.; XES-40S3, 1-sun) with magnetic stirring. In most cases, visible light (λ > 420 nm) was irradiated using an NaNO2 (20 wt%) solution filter [17]. In some cases, entire wavelength light (λ > 300 nm) or λ > 450 nm light was irradiated without filter or with a CS3-72 glass filter (Kopp Glass Inc.) [18]. The amounts of H2O2 in the solutions were determined by the redox titration with KMnO4.

Electrochemical analysis was carried out in a three-electrode cell using an electrochemical analyzer (ALS700E, BAS Inc.). The Ag/AgCl and Pt wire electrodes were used as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively, and the catalyst-loaded glassy carbon (GC) electrodes were used as the working electrodes. The working electrodes were prepared as follows [16]: catalyst (20 mg) was added to a mixture of water (4 mL) and 2-propanol (1.5 mL), and ultrasonicated for 10 min. The slurry was placed onto a GC electrode and dried at room temperature. The film was formed to 0.5 cm × 0.5 cm square, where the non-deposited parts were coated with an epoxy resin [6]. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were performed at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1 after the N2 bubbling through the solution for 1 h. Diffuse-reflectance ultraviolet–visible (DR UV–vis), powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Mg-Kα radiation) analyses were described in our previous work [16,19]. Scanning transmission electron microscopy–energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (STEM–EDS) observations were conducted on a JEOL JEM-ARM200F microscope at 200 kV [8]. The total elemental analysis for C, H, and N was performed using an organic elements analyzer JM10 CHN (J-Science Lab), while the F and S elements were analyzed using an organic elements analysis system XS-2100H (Mitsubishi Chemical Analytech).

The QD/CN powder was prepared by the hydrothermal treatment of CN in a QDs solution [12]. The designated volume (x mL) of a Nf dispersion solution (5 wt%) was added to water containing the QD/CN powder (0.4 g). Stirring the mixture at room temperature for 24 h and subsequent evaporation of the solvents afforded the Nf x -QD/CN catalysts. As shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary material), the powder XRD of CN, QD/CN, and Nf x -QD/CN shows a peak assigned to the (002) packing of the CN sheet at the identical position [2θ = 27.4° (d = 0.325 nm)] [20], indicating that these catalysts maintain the layered stacking structure of CN even after the Nf coating. As shown in Fig. S2 (Supplementary material), the QD/CN shows a strong bandgap absorption of CN at λ < 450 nm and a broader absorption of QD at λ > 450 nm [12]. The Nf coating scarcely change the spectra, which agrees with almost no absorption of Nf at λ > 300 nm (Fig. S3, Supplementary material).

Photocatalytic H2O2 splitting was performed in a closed gas circulation system at a reduced pressure (3 kPa Ar). Visible light (λ > 420 nm) was irradiated by a solar simulator with an AM 1.5 G filter (1-sun) to a H2O2 solution (1 mmol, 0.1 L) containing catalyst (0.2 g) at 293 K. Table 1 lists the amounts of H2 and O2 generated and the amounts of H2O2 consumed during 6 h of photoirradiation. The QD/CN (entry 1) does not produce H2 because the absence of H3PO4 promotes the H2O2 reduction (Eq. (7)) and suppresses the H+ reduction (Eq. (4)) [12]. Addition of H3PO4 to the solution generates H2 (entry 2), indicating that the H2O2–H3PO4 hydrogen-bonding interaction suppresses the H2O2 reduction and facilitates the H+ reduction [12]. In contrast, the Nf x -QD/CN catalysts (entries 3–5) generate H2 even without H3PO4, suggesting that the Nf coating is indeed effective to suppress the H2O2 reduction. Note that the activity depends on the amounts of Nf coated: Nf0.4-QD/CN (entry 4) exhibits the highest activity, and the coating with excess amounts of Nf generates almost no H2 (entries 6, 7). In addition, as shown by entry 8, an Nf dispersion solution, when added to the H2O2 solution containing QD/CN, does not produce H2. This indicates that the surface coverage by the appropriate amount of Nf is crucial for H2 generation. As shown by entry 9, the Nf0.4-QD/CN catalyst in the dark scarcely generate H2 and O2 with almost no decomposition of H2O2, as is the case without Nf coating [12]. In addition, as shown by entry 10, photoirradiation of the catalyst at a longer wavelength light (λ > 450 nm) also scarcely produce H2, indicating that the broad QD absorption (Fig. S2, Supplementary material) does not contribute to the photocatalysis. Furthermore, as shown by entry 11, photoirradiation of entire wavelength light from the solar simulator (λ > 300 nm) generates larger amount of H2 (2.2 μmol) than the case with λ > 420 nm light irradiation (1.0 μmol, entry 4) because of the enhanced light absorption of the catalyst. However, in this case, very large amount of H2O2 (286 μmol) is consumed during the reaction as compared to the case with λ > 420 nm light irradiation (64 μmol, entry 4) because of the photodecomposition of H2O2 by absorbing UV light (Eq. (6)). These results indicate that visible light irradiation is necessary for selective H2O2 splitting.

Electrochemical analysis was performed to clarify the role of the Nf coated on the QD/CN surface. Fig. 1 shows the LSV curves obtained with the respective catalysts loaded on a glassy carbon (GC) electrode in 0.1 M H2O2 solution containing 0.1 M Na2SO4 as an electrolyte. As shown by the black line in Fig. 1a, the QD/CN exhibits a strong cathodic current assigned to the H2O2 reduction (Eq. (7)) at < 0.1 V (vs. the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE)), as confirmed by the LSV measurements without H2O2 (Fig. S4, Supplementary material). In contrast, as shown by the red and blue lines, the intensity of this current decreases with the amounts of Nf coated. This indicates that the coated Nf inhibits the permeation of H2O2 as well as water [15] and, hence, suppresses the H2O2 reduction on the QDs. Therefore, the H2O2-impermeable/H+-permeable property of the surface Nf facilitates H+ reduction on the QDs and generates H2 on the Nf x -QD/CN catalysts (Table 1, entries 3–5). As shown in Fig. 1b, the LSV curves obtained at a positive potential exhibit an anodic current assigned to the H2O2 oxidation (Eq. (3)) at > 0.7 V. As shown by the red and blue line, the intensity of this current decreases with the amounts of Nf coated because the H2O2-impermeable property of Nf also suppresses the H2O2 oxidation on the CN surface. This indicates that, as shown by entries 6, 7 in Table 1, the excess amounts of the Nf coated may suppress the H2O2 oxidation, thus decreasing the photocatalytic activity. Coating with appropriate amounts of Nf is therefore necessary for efficient H2 generation.

The structure of the active catalyst (Nf0.4-QD/CN) was characterized. Table 2 summarizes the results of elemental analysis of the catalysts. The Nf-coated catalysts show the F and S elements for the PTFE [= poly(tetrafluoroethylene)] backbone and sulfonate side chains, respectively, and these compositions increase proportionally to the amounts of the Nf dispersion solution used during the Nf coating. This indicates that many of the Nf added are successfully loaded on the QD/CN surface. The Nf0.4-QD/CN catalyst recovered after the photoreaction (Table 1, entry 4) exhibits the F and S amounts almost similar to those of the fresh one (within ± 20 % errors). The strong adhesion of Nf onto the QD/CN surface may involve the interaction between the anionic sulfonate moieties of Nf and the positively charged N atoms of CN [21], as observed in Nf-coated TiO2 systems [22]. In addition, as shown in Figs. S1 and S2 (Supplementary material), the XRD pattern and DR UV–vis spectrum of the recovered Nf0.4-QD/CN catalyst are almost unchanged. Furthermore, as shown in Table 1 (entry 4), the recovered catalyst, when reused for the repeated photoreactions, exhibits almost the same activity as the fresh one, suggesting that the catalyst maintains its activity even after the repeated reactions.

The surface states of the catalysts were characterized by the STEM-EDS observations. Note that rapid and low-magnification scan is necessary for the present Nf-QD/CN catalysts because the F components evaporate quickly upon irradiation of the electron beam. Fig. 2 shows the TEM images and the EDS maps of the C and F elements of the QD/CN, Nf0.4-QD/CN, and Nf1.6-QD/CN catalysts. The QD/CN (left) are amorphous particles with ∼ 1 μm sizes loaded with some QDs with ∼ 20 nm diameters [12]. Its EDS map indicates that the C elements shown by the red color are homogeneously distributed over the entire CN particles. In this case, light green coloring is observed despite the absence of F elements, which originates from the high bottom baseline of the EDS spectrum (Fig. S5, Supplementary material). In the case of Nf1.6-QD/CN (right), intensely colored C and F elements are homogeneously distributed over the entire particles (see detail: Fig. S6, Supplementary material). This indicates that a large area of the QD/CN surface is covered by Nf, creating the surface Nf layer. In contrast, the F element map of Nf0.4-QD/CN (center, Fig. 2) is a mixture of intensely and lightly colored areas (see detail: Fig. S7, Supplementary material). As shown in Figs. S8–10 (Supplementary material), other Nf0.4-QD/CN particles also exhibit similar color distribution with shades. These observations indicate that the active catalysts have a part covered with Nf and a part not covered with Nf.

XPS was used for further characterization of the catalyst surfaces (Fig. S11, Supplementary material). As shown in Fig. S12 (Supplementary material), the C 1 s spectra of the catalysts show strong peaks for the N = C–N components of the triazine ring of CN (287.8 eV) [23] and the graphitic C = C components of QDs (284.6 eV). The Nf0.4-QD/CN and Nf1.6-QD/CN catalysts also exhibit the CF2 components of Nf (291.5 eV). The Nf-coated catalysts show the corresponding F 1 s (Fig. S13, Supplementary material) and S 2p (Fig. S14, Supplementary material) peaks [24]. The N 1 s spectra of all catalysts (Fig. S15, Supplementary material) show two components assigned to the pyridinic N atoms of melem (398.5 eV) and trigonal N atoms of melem and tertiary amines of melem terminal (399.5 eV) with similar area ratios (6/5). It is noted that the intensity of these N components decreases with the amounts of Nf coated. This indicates that the penetration of the electron beam is suppressed by the coated Nf, although the penetration depth in the Nf layer is unclear. The surface elemental compositions (C, F, S, N, and O) of the catalysts can be determined by their XPS peak areas (Fig. S12–S16, Supplementary material) using the atomic sensitivity factors [25]. As summarized in Table 3 , the surface N compositions of QD/CN, Nf0.4-QD/CN, and Nf1.6-QD/CN are 51.1 mol %, 35.9 mol %, and 20.0 mol %, respectively, confirming the coverage of the QD/CN surface by Nf. Based on the N compositions, the percent exposure of the QD/CN surface on the catalysts can roughly be calculated by the following equation:
(8)
e x p o s u r e
%
=
N
c o m p o s i t i o n
o f
t h e
c a t a l y s t s
[ m o l
% ]
N
c o m p o s i t i o n
o f
Q D / C N
( 51.09 )
× 100

The exposure of the QD/CN surface on the respective catalysts was determined to be 100 % (QD/CN), 70 % (Nf0.4-QD/CN), and 39 % (Nf1.6-QD/CN), respectively, although the thickness of the Nf layers is unclear. These data clearly indicate that partial Nf coverage is necessary. Note that the percent exposure of the Nf0.4-QD/CN catalyst recovered after the photoreaction is similar to that of the fresh one (Table 3), indicating that the Nf stably coats the QD/CN surface, as confirmed by the elemental analysis (Table 2).

The reaction mechanism on the respective catalysts can be explained as Fig. 3 . The photoformed VB h+ are localized on the CN, and the CB e– are localized on the QDs. Without Nf (Fig. 3a), the CB e– readily reduce H2O2 (Eq. (7)) and inhibits the H+ reduction. However, as shown in Fig. 3b, partial surface coverage with an appropriate amount of Nf randomly creates exposed/coated surfaces (∼70 % surface exposure). On the Nf-covered QDs, the H2O2-impermeable/H+-permeable property of Nf facilitates the H+ reduction and successfully produces H2. In contrast, as shown in Fig. 3c, further coverage with excess amounts of Nf decreases the exposed CN surface (∼40 % surface exposure). This suppresses the H2O2 oxidation by the VB h+ and results in inefficient generation of H2. The partial coverage of the QD/CN surface (∼70% surface exposure) with appropriate amounts of Nf is therefore necessary.

Fig. 4 represents the time-dependent change in the amounts of H2 generated and the H2 selectivity during photoirradiation of Nf0.4-QD/CN in a H2O2 solution or photoirradiation of QD/CN in a H2O2 solution with H3PO4 [12], where the H2 selectivity is the ratio of the amount of H2 generated to that of H2O2 consumed, as follows:
(9)
H 2
s e l e c v i t y
%
=
H 2
e v o l v e d
[ μ m o l ]
H 2
O 2
c o n s u m e d
[ μ m o l ]
× 100

The amount of H2 produced in the Nf0.4-QD/CN system increases almost linearly with the photoirradiation time, as is the case of the QD/CN–H3PO4 system [12]. The H2 selectivity is almost constant during the photoirradiation. The selectivity (∼1.5 %) is, however, very low, which is much lower than that in the QD/CN–H3PO4 system (∼6.0 %) [12]. This indicates that very large amount of H2O2 is consumed as compared to the amount of H2 evolved, although the coated Nf suppresses the H2O2 permeation to the CN surface. As shown in Fig. 3b (right), the QD/CN surface is randomly covered by Nf, where uncovered QDs also exist. These uncovered QDs may inevitably promote the H2O2 reduction and, hence, decreases the H2 selectivity. It is also noted that a relatively low temperature (∼293 K) is necessary for the H2 generation on the Nf0.4-QD/CN system: the activity decreases at higher temperatures (Fig. S17, Supplementary material). Nf undergoes swelling at higher temperature [26]. This may also allow the H2O2 penetration, probably promoting the H2O2 reduction on the QD surface.

We demonstrated that visible light irradiation of the partially-Nf-coated QD/CN catalyst could generate H2 by the H2O2 splitting. The Nf layer created on the QD/CN surface exhibits the H2O2-impermeable/H+-permeable property and promotes the H+ reduction on the QDs while suppressing the H2O2 reduction. The high activity is achieved on the catalyst with ∼70 % surface exposure, while the lower-exposure-catalysts show decreased activity. The present random Nf coverage inevitably creates uncoated QDs; they promote the H2O2 reduction and decrease the selectivity for H2 generation. Therefore, a new method to selectively coat Nf onto the QDs surface is necessary for further activity/selectivity improvement. Nevertheless, the present photocatalytic system based on a simple Nf coating using a commercially available Nf dispersion solution may contribute to the design of efficient system for the on-site generation of H2 from a H2O2 solution.

Yasuhiro Shiraishi: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Airu Soramoto: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Satoshi Ichikawa: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shunsuke Tanaka: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Takayuki Hirai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Grant-in-Aid for Scientific Research on Innovative Areas (20H05100) and Challenging Exploratory Research (20K21109) and Nanotechnology Platform Project (Nanotechnology Open Facilities in Osaka University) (JPMXP09A20OS0032 and JPMXP09A21OS0005) from the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology, Japan (MEXT).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.113949.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
